[32](https://rateyourmusic.com/release/album/bon-iver/for-emma-forever-ago/reviews/32/) [>>](https://rateyourmusic.com/release/album/bon-iver/for-emma-forever-ago/reviews/2/)

[User resist_retreat](https://rateyourmusic.com/~resist_retreat "User resist_retreat") [resist\_retreat](https://rateyourmusic.com/~resist_retreat)[Feb 28 2011](https://rateyourmusic.com/music-review/resist_retreat/bon-iver/for-emma-forever-ago/36045598)![3.00 stars](https://cdn.sonemic.net/2.5/img/images/6m.png)

Somewhere in the basement, hopefully ravaged by cold and damp, I have a succession of notebooks containing any mumber of thoughts, ideas, events and the like from my past. Far too much is terribly mawkish and self-pitying.

Ever since acoustic introspection became one of the most praised qualities in music over a decade ago, I've been quietly pleased that I was never tempted to attempt to publish any of my musings. If nothing else, the age/time continuum and thinner hair has taught me that however acute private feelings may be, they do not make for great art unless they can be embedded in a wider context.

In the old authenticity discourse of popular music, Bon Iver ticks all the right boxes. Personal crisis transformed into soul-baring music in the confines of a log cabin in the middle of nowhere. What interests me is how authenticity continues to be such a cherished notion when surely it has become increasingly clear (in the age of "reality" TV) that nothing ultimately destined for public consumption can be created without some kind of imagined consumer, and that the anticipated meeting of record with listener necessarily adulterates the purity of the artist's emotional outpouring to begin with.

So I approached _For Emma, Forever Ago_ with a deep level of suspicion and very few expectations whatsoever.

I was surprised. I don't really care about Iver's personal travails. I don't mean to sound hard-hearted, but I don't know the man and there's no reason to suppose that his life's story is any more interesting than that of random people you happen to pass on the street. The fact that he had the good fortune to make a record has nothing to do with any potential universality of his experience compared to anybody else's.

No, what surprised me was the level of warmth expressed through these songs - even when Iver's at his most vindictive. This kind of odd juxtaposition always pleases me. It is a warmth that suggests transcendence rather than the kind of constipation normally implied by this kind of music. At times I was even put in mind of [XTC](https://rateyourmusic.com/artist/xtc "[Artist63]")'s _[Apple Venus Volume 1](https://rateyourmusic.com/release/album/xtc/apple-venus-volume-1/)_, which is praise indeed.

Having said that, you can have too much of a good thing. Iver's weakness is one that he shares with almost all of the folksy singer-songwriters of the past 20 years; if you are going to pull off the minimalism of the genre, you need something really striking to lift you ahead of the herd, and Iver really doesn't have the personality for that - or at least not one that comes across here.

What I really wish is that Iver had left his introspective catharsis to rot in the basement, transforming it into some kind of epiphany that had more relevance beyond the strictly personal. There's promise here, but it goes unfulfilled.

Published

ADVERTISEMENT

[User TheCunningStunt](https://rateyourmusic.com/~TheCunningStunt "User TheCunningStunt") [TheCunningStunt](https://rateyourmusic.com/~TheCunningStunt)[Feb 19 2024](https://rateyourmusic.com/music-review/TheCunningStunt/bon-iver/for-emma-forever-ago/41294949)![5.00 stars](https://cdn.sonemic.net/2.5/img/images/10m.png)

Love, human interaction, connection, relationships. It really is a strange thing, but something that we're almost inevitably drawn to due to human nature and a human need. It's something that's incredibly bittersweet because, for me, love inevitably ends. Even if you stay with a person until you die, it doesn't mean that the adoration, the initial connection, the lust, that smitten feeling where you're just infatuated by that person, that ineffable feeling that's so incredibly intense and profound will remain the same.

It's not impossible and I'm sure, or at least, I hope it can happen. I hope that idealised vision of romantic love where you adore each other til the very end is something that's obtainable, but I just don't believe it is. It's likely that you'll split up. People are annoying, their habits are annoying, spending too much time in another person's company can be annoying. People change, people get bored, people have irritating habits, people feel trapped and claustrophobic.

However, the pain, sadness, heartache, regret, longing in the end is worth it. Because for a period of time, somebody else understood you, related to you, you connected, they loved you, were intimate with you. Love can change the way you look at the world. It can make you feel a certain way that's incredibly hard to define, you just feel like you're a better person because you're spending time with a particular person. Falling in love is one of the best things that can happen to a human being, falling out of love or watching your relationship disintegrate is one of the worst things that can happen to you.

\-\-\-----

**Review:**

_For Emma, Forever Ago_ is one of few albums that absolutely destroys me on an emotional and visceral level, it's an incredibly personal and important album to me. What Bon Iver managed to do was create his own immersive little world, and the only thing that inhibits that world is the sound of loneliness, isolation and heartbreak. It's an absolute masterpiece. It truly is one of the best albums ever made and I think that's partly due to Justin Vernon managing to create his own incredibly realised little world here and every time I listen to the album, I just get lost in that world. It also has the songs, the atmosphere, the rich and interesting back story, the critical acclaim and the following. All of its acclaim and adoration is definitely deserved. A lot of people say that they don't make 'em like they used to, this is one of many examples of why that's just not true.

A broken and ill Justin Vernon secluded himself in a cabin in the northwestern Wisconsin for three months. He had been going through a tough time, and he wanted complete solitude. The result was _For Emma, Forever Ago_. He made his heartache audible. As you can probably tell from the name of the album, his heartache was largely due to a girl. Emma. Even though it has an interesting backstory, there's still an awful lot of mystery, mystique and ambiguity to it. I always wonder to myself what went through Emma's mind when she listened to her former lover pour his heart out over her. It's an incredibly compelling and fascinating story, a fantastic foundation for an album.

Having the foundation would mean very little if Justin Vernon didn't have the ability and talent to execute the album he was trying to make. When you're making this kind of intimate music, I think there's quite a set formula and it's a relatively easy one to implement but to make something truly special, you need something more. Something that transcends the technical aspects and the production. It has to have the feeling, it has to feel sincere and heartfelt - and _For Emma, Forever Ago_ is most definitely that. It's some of the most heartfelt and deeply personal music I've ever listened to. I think that's the most important thing when making this kind of album, though musically it's stunning. The soft warm wispy acoustic guitar is perfect, and it creates a wonderful atmosphere. The vocal harmonies are beautiful as well and there's a lot of subtlety as well, like sparse use of horns and electronic effects. It's not as one dimensional as you'd think, it has more sonic depth than I was expecting yet it simultaneously manages to stay incredibly stripped down and minimalistic.

This is the sound of icy cold glacial loneliness. It's the perfect album to listen to in winter, I find it weirdly comforting to listen to and if you've ever had your heart broken, you can relate to it. _For Emma, Forever Ago_ is desolate, poignant, introspective, deep, beautiful, chilling, intimate, delicate and vulnerable. So full of woe and longing, it's an album that really hits you hard. Justin Vernon really does create his own little world that's so full of isolation and pain, yet it so easy to get lost in.

\-\-\-----

Hopefully whatever relationship you're in lasts and hopefully that person completes you and will do so until the day you die. But if the worst does happen and once that love does dissolve and disintegrate into nothing. Nothing other than intangible things like memories, regret and longing and whilst you're going through that phase where you feel empty, lost, alone and like you're claustrophobic and suffocating. You turn to the things that matter most to you, the things that can kick you out of anhedonia. Even though life is ultimately nihilistic and nothing, including your romantic relationships will matter in the end, you turn to the things that matter most to you whilst you're alive. Things like music, film, literature, friends, alcohol. You know, the natural break up remedies. I have always turned to a select few albums, this is one of them.

No matter what the relationship, you will always feel the tiniest bit better, the slightest bit comforted when you listen to _For Emma, Forever Ago_. It's as close to perfect as music gets. It's a personal, introspective, beautiful, heartfelt, insightful and honest work that I get lost and immersed in every single time.

Published

I realize finally adding the scores to this review eliminates some of its simplistic beauty but, it's about time I figure.

\[Dec 06 2010\]

I had an amazing review written for this, with scores and everything, and then I clicked the delete button and for whatever reason my browser switched to another page and I lost everything. This has happened to me before but it was really, really important to me right now. The lesson is, sometimes technology sucks and it is just best to go off by yourself and get your thoughts down in a location where everything is secure and under your own control. Sometimes things turn out best that way.

Published

[User Grampus](https://rateyourmusic.com/~Grampus "User Grampus") [Grampus](https://rateyourmusic.com/~Grampus)[Jun 01 2010](https://rateyourmusic.com/music-review/Grampus/bon-iver/for-emma-forever-ago-2/15240239)![2.00 stars](https://cdn.sonemic.net/2.5/img/images/4m.png)

Just partway through the opening track "Flume" I began to fear the worse: was Bon Iver going to be another case of the Jeff Buckleys? Over the years I've received a great deal of criticism for my lack of appreciation for Buckley – some of it from users of this site. My stance has always been you simply can't like everybody, but the level of adulation for Buckley has always struck me as way too excessive, verging on the unhealthy. When _For Emma, Forever Ago_ was released the furore it created reminded me very much of that surrounding _Grace_ – and the lasting personal effect is virtually the same.

Bon Iver, aka Justin Vernon, devastated by a combination of the break-up of his previous band, a debilitating illness and the disintegration of a personal relationship, locked himself away in a remote cabin in the woods of Wisconsin and composed the songs for the album in what appears to be a cathartic rush. As a means of recovery, the recuperative power of isolation can't be overstated but I'm not convinced the results are something which needed to be shared. Speaking from experience, when you've been dumped or feeling under the weather the tendency is to wallow in self-pity, taking a swipe at anything and everything you believe is to blame. Sometimes the object of disaffection is yourself, sometimes those perceived to have done you wrong but, in either case, it's far too easy to sound whiny and vindictive as here with the opening lines of "Wolves": "Someday my pain, someday my pain will mark you. Harness your blame, harness your blame and walk through."

It also doesn't help matters that Iver's voice quivers like a highly emotional bowstring. The timbre of a vocal very rarely irritates but there are moments when fingernails scraping down a blackboard would be infinitely preferable. Equally perplexing is Iver's overall approach to songwriting. Apparently he would develop and refine the music and then formulate words to fit the melody. The lyrics are not exactly stream of consciousness but sometimes the dots are difficult to join up.

Yet there are occasions when full potential is realised and everything comes together. "Skinny Love", "For Emma" and "Re: Stacks" are the best of the bunch but are not strong enough to salvage a disappointing and one-paced collection.

Maybe I'm being unduly harsh but _For Emma, Forever Ago_ fails to leave any sort of impression and I won't be buying anything else by Bon Iver without a prior listen. I'm not as cold as when hearing _Grace_ but I do feel decidedly chilly.

Published
